original_line: Yes, I'd like to speak to your office manager, please.
core_expression: I'd like to speak to ___, please.
meaning_kr: 담당자와 통화하고 싶을 때 정중하게 요청하는 표현
examples: I'd like to speak to the person in charge of shipping, please.
I'd like to speak to your manager about a billing issue.
I'd like to speak to someone from HR, please.
I'd like to speak to the account owner directly.
I'd like to speak to whoever handles vendor contracts.

original_line: Yes hello, this is Michael Scott, I am the regional manager of Dunder Mifflin Paper products.
core_expression: This is [이름], I am the [직책] of [회사/부서].
meaning_kr: 전화에서 이름과 직책을 소개하는 정형화된 표현
examples: This is Sarah Kim, I am the marketing manager at ABC Corp.
This is John Lee, I'm the head of the sales team.
This is Grace Park, I'm the project lead for the new launch.
This is David, I'm the account manager handling your order.
This is Mina, I am the customer support supervisor.

original_line: I've been in Dunder Mifflin for twelve years, the last four as regional manager.
core_expression: I've been at/in [회사] for [기간], the last [기간] as [직책].
meaning_kr: 재직 기간과 최근 직책 경력을 소개할 때 쓰는 표현
examples: I've been at this company for five years, the last two as team lead.
She's been with the firm for a decade, most recently as VP of Sales.
I've been in this industry for eight years, the last three in project management.
He's been at the bank for six years, the last one as branch manager.
I've worked here for three years, the last year leading the design team.

original_line: I think that pretty much sums it up.
core_expression: I think that pretty much sums it up.
meaning_kr: 설명을 마무리하며 '대충 이 정도로 요약된다'고 말할 때
examples: I think that pretty much sums up our Q3 performance.
That pretty much sums up the whole project timeline.
I think that sums it up for today's meeting.
That pretty much covers everything we discussed.
I think that sums up my main concerns.

original_line: My job is to speak to clients on the phone about quantities and type of copier paper.
core_expression: My job is to [업무 내용].
meaning_kr: 자신의 업무를 간단히 설명할 때 쓰는 표현
examples: My job is to manage the budget and coordinate with vendors.
My job is to review contracts before they're signed.
Her job is to handle customer inquiries and resolve complaints.
My job is to oversee the production schedule.
His job is to analyze market trends and report findings.

original_line: Was there anything you wanted to add to the agenda?
core_expression: Was there anything you wanted to add to ___?
meaning_kr: 회의 시작 전 안건 추가 여부를 확인할 때 쓰는 표현
examples: Was there anything you wanted to add to the agenda before we start?
Is there anything you'd like to add to the discussion?
Was there anything else you wanted to bring up today?
Is there anything you want to add before we wrap up?
Was there anything you wanted to cover that we missed?

original_line: Well I faxed one over to you this morning.
core_expression: I faxed/sent ___ over to you this morning.
meaning_kr: 오전에 자료를 전달했다고 알릴 때 (이메일 등으로 응용 가능)
examples: I faxed the contract over to you this morning.
I emailed the report over to you this morning.
I sent the invoice over to accounting this morning.
I forwarded the file over to the team this morning.
I dropped the documents off at your desk this morning.

original_line: You put it in the garbage can, that was a special filing cabinet.
core_expression: That was a special ___.
meaning_kr: 상대가 물건/자료를 잘못 처리했음을 정정할 때
examples: You deleted the file, but that was a special backup copy.
You canceled the order, but that was a special custom request.
You archived the folder, but that was our active project file.
You returned the item, but that was a limited edition.
You threw away the draft, but that was the final approved version.

original_line: Since the last meeting, Ellan and the board have decided that we can't justify the Scranton branch and the Stanford branch.
core_expression: Since the last meeting, [주체] have decided that ___.
meaning_kr: 지난 회의 이후 결정된 사항을 공지할 때
examples: Since the last meeting, the board has decided that we can't justify the current budget.
Since our last call, management has decided to postpone the launch.
Since the last review, the team has decided to change the approach.
Since the last meeting, HR has decided to revise the policy.
Since the last discussion, the client has decided to go with another vendor.

original_line: It's up to either you or him to convince me that your branch can incorporate the other.
core_expression: It's up to [주체] to convince me that ___.
meaning_kr: 상대에게 설득의 책임이 있음을 말할 때
examples: It's up to you to convince the client that this plan works.
It's up to either of you to convince me this is worth the investment.
It's up to the team to convince management to approve the budget.
It's up to you to convince him to join the project.
It's up to her to convince the board this merger makes sense.

original_line: I don't think it would be the worst thing if they let me go.
core_expression: I don't think it would be the worst thing if ___.
meaning_kr: 최악은 아닐 것 같다고 담담하게 말할 때
examples: I don't think it would be the worst thing if we delayed the launch by a week.
I don't think it would be the worst thing if we scaled back the budget.
I don't think it would be the worst thing if he left the project.
I don't think it would be the worst thing if we outsourced this task.
I don't think it would be the worst thing if the meeting got shorter.

original_line: I just, I don't think it's many girls' dream to be a receptionist.
core_expression: I don't think it's many people's dream to ___.
meaning_kr: 어떤 일이 흔히 꿈꾸는 일은 아니라고 말할 때
examples: I don't think it's many people's dream to work overtime every day.
I don't think it's everyone's dream to work in sales.
I don't think it's her dream to stay in this role forever.
I don't think commuting two hours is anyone's dream.
I don't think it's a common dream to manage a call center.

original_line: I heard they might be closing this branch down.
core_expression: I heard they might be [동사-ing] ___.
meaning_kr: 소문이나 추측으로 들은 정보를 전할 때
examples: I heard they might be closing this branch down.
I heard they might be restructuring the team next quarter.
I heard they might be cutting the marketing budget.
I heard they might be hiring a new director soon.
I heard they might be moving the office downtown.

original_line: Sometimes we play hard when we should be working hard, right?
core_expression: Sometimes we [행동A] when we should be [행동B].
meaning_kr: 해야 할 일 대신 다른 걸 하고 있음을 인정할 때
examples: Sometimes we chat when we should be working, right?
Sometimes we procrastinate when we should be preparing for the deadline.
Sometimes we overthink when we should just make a decision.
Sometimes we cut corners when we should be double-checking.
Sometimes we multitask when we should be focusing on one thing.

original_line: As you know, there is going to be downsizing.
core_expression: As you know, there is going to be ___.
meaning_kr: 이미 알려진 변화나 계획을 공식적으로 언급할 때
examples: As you know, there is going to be a restructuring next month.
As you know, there is going to be a system upgrade this weekend.
As you know, there is going to be a price increase next quarter.
As you know, there is going to be a merger with our partner company.
As you know, there is going to be a change in leadership.

original_line: You have made my life so much easier in that I'm going to have to let you go first.
core_expression: You've made [상황] easier in that ___.
meaning_kr: 상황이 오히려 쉬워졌음을 (역설적으로) 설명할 때
examples: You've made this decision easier in that we now have the data we need.
You've made my job easier in that you always double-check your work.
You've made the transition easier in that you documented everything.
You've made the negotiation easier in that you were upfront about the budget.
You've made onboarding easier in that you prepared a clear guide.

original_line: What am I supposed to have stolen? Post-it Notes.
core_expression: What am I supposed to have [과거분사]?
meaning_kr: 부당한 혐의나 추궁에 억울함을 표현할 때
examples: What am I supposed to have done wrong here?
What am I supposed to have missed in the report?
What am I supposed to have said that upset the client?
What am I supposed to have approved without checking?
What am I supposed to have broken exactly?

original_line: You steal a thousand Post-it notes at 50 cents a piece, then you know you made a profit margin.
core_expression: You [행동] at [단가], then you [결과].
meaning_kr: 단가나 수량을 근거로 계산 결과를 설명할 때
examples: You sell a thousand units at $5 a piece, then you've covered the production cost.
You buy in bulk at a discount, then you save on shipping too.
You price it at $20 a unit, then the margin works out fine.
You charge per hour instead of per project, then billing gets simpler.
You order early at a lower rate, then you lock in the savings.

original_line: I have never even stolen as much as a paper clip and now you're firing me.
core_expression: I have never even [과거분사] as much as ___.
meaning_kr: 아주 사소한 것조차 한 적 없다고 강하게 부인할 때
examples: I have never even missed a deadline, let alone a whole project.
I have never even raised my voice, let alone been rude to a client.
I have never even taken an extra minute of lunch, let alone left early.
I have never even used a company card for personal use, not once.
I have never even been late, let alone absent without notice.

original_line: Surprise! It was a joke. We were joking around. See? He was on it, he was my accomplice.
core_expression: It was a joke. We were joking around.
meaning_kr: 상황을 반전시키며 농담이었다고 밝힐 때
examples: Surprise! It was a joke, we were just testing the new system.
It was a joke, we were just messing with the presentation slides.
We were joking around about the deadline, don't worry, it's real.
It was a joke, I was just kidding about canceling the meeting.
Surprise! We were just joking, the budget cut isn't real.

original_line: My proudest moment here was not when I increased profits by 17%...
core_expression: My proudest moment was not when ___, but when ___.
meaning_kr: 성과보다 더 자랑스러운 순간이 따로 있음을 강조할 때
examples: My proudest moment here was not when I closed the biggest deal.
My proudest moment wasn't when we hit the sales target.
His proudest moment wasn't when he got promoted.
Our proudest moment wasn't when we launched the product on time.
My proudest moment wasn't when I won the award.

original_line: ...or when I cut expenses without losing a single employee.
core_expression: [성과] without losing a single ___.
meaning_kr: 손실 없이 성과를 냈음을 강조할 때
examples: We cut expenses without losing a single employee.
We hit the deadline without losing a single client.
We restructured the team without losing a single key member.
We migrated the system without losing a single record.
We downsized the office without losing a single major account.
